item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The quantizer and the entangler seem pretty creepy and I wouldn’t ever want to even go near one of them for fear of what would happen

But the refractor though

Guys do you have any idea how much crap I could get done with that thing? Bro I don’t even need to know how quantum refraction works, the second test with the doctor means that as long as I don’t think too hard about it and have an existential crisis I can just obliterate studying for all of my college finals simultaneously. I mean I already have migraines, who cares. Sign me up

Also… yeah the story is pretty good. The bit with the teachers reaction feels very organic. These are some things that aren’t outright terrifying but are very much something you don’t want using with kids around

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this a lot. The escalation of the mini horror stories, all tied into a larger concept appeals to me; the banal intention behind these devices and the ways they all go spectacularly wrong, the very human reactions to the wrongness, the quite horrific image of the kid getting zapped after we've seen how problematic the process is, all very effective. One of the better "geez these fun products are no fun" pieces I've seen, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi Riemann,

Thank you for your insight and suggestions!

I have tried to address as many of your tonal comments as possible in the current draft of the article (D-2413, D-1211, and D-2834 becoming subjects, glass breaking to glass fracturing, etc.) but have left the main thread of the tests the same. I received a lot of feedback that the way the logs were written let the ideas become accessible without a defined description of the properties themselves.

I had not considered the Factory model and it had not come up in critique before! I have added it in in the way of http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-4876 as a Wondertainment product manufactured by the Factory. I really like this suggestion and thank you for it!

Thank you so much for your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is, on the whole, competently done. There are no glaring tonal breaks (we'll get to the test logs), and I've left with a good understanding of what is going on and what you wanted to get across.

+ That being said, there are a couple things that prevent me from giving this an upvote.
- Bottom text

1

The testing logs drag. I found all of them difficult to get through, and they didn't particularly add anything after the first example. I gather I'm supposed to be left wondering about the quantiser, for instance, and what its internal logic is, but… I don't, I'm afraid.

Four steps after walking through the door, D-2413, D-1211, and D-2834 all stop walking and form a triangle facing in the same direction. D-2413, D-1211, and D-2834 remain silent for 60 seconds before the research team prompts them with a question. D-2413, D-1211, and D-2834…

The repetition of the D-class make this entire thing difficult to parse. The interview log format for -3 is genuinely clever - but again, it's difficult to parse.

And the descriptions in -2 are… weird. It feels like an attempt to balance formal tone with conveying something fantastical, but it ends up being stilted and odd.

The space inside the room appeared to fracture, reminiscent of glass breaking, and then returned to the appearance of the room as it was before activation of SCP-5367-2.

This is a wild sentence and is exemplary of the overall reliance on "appeared" in the text. I sympathise - describing that "glass breaking" without using the term itself is hard, but you end up straddling this line between "official transcription of visuals" and what is clearly an artistic description.

2

This doesn't feel like a Wondertainment product to me. These can't really be construed as "fun", in any capacity. Where's the childish wonder? As presented these aren't toys, they're tools. They're not something to play with, they're something to, well, stop people from having fun. I think a more appropriate Wondertainment angle would be some "edutainment" toy that superpositions the child using it in some horrifying and disorienting manner. But as it is this is almost more of a Factory toy to me.

3

This is relatively minor, but this toy interacts with Quantum Mechanics principles on such a surface level. I do enjoy the Quantiser - I think it's a cute joke and feels fairly appropriate for Wondertainment. But the Entangler and Diffractor, so much less so. "Diffractor splits things so therefore we gotta split them and do this superpositioning thing". I know this is my bugbear, I do, but there's so much more that could be done with these.

4

The discovery log and blog post at the end are extraneous. You spent the whole article not talking about this teacher, so for them to just show up again breaks the flow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this and what you have. One of the things I like is the blog post since it adds believe-ness and realism to it, like if someone got their hands on it, that may be how they react.

I have some nitpicks and critiques for this article though:

+ show block
– hide block

Follow up with D-1526 D-1526 expressed that, from their perspective, …

I think there's supposed to be a colon there.

I'd suggest having a blank line between some paragraphs at certain points. An example of what I mean:

D-1527 The device acted on D-1527 as observed in prior testing and presented the number 0 after completing the quantization process. Upon being returned to the room by SCP-5367-1, D-1527’s epidermal layer sloughed off and subject began to seize. D-1527 expired after 14 minutes on continuous seizing and blood loss.

Researcher note: It appears, after re-watching security footage during Test 4, that a fruit fly entered the space between the aperture and D-1527. Based on the disastrous outcome of test 4, we ask that all further testing with SCP-5367-1 be ensured to have a free field of view between the subject and SCP-5367-1.

Researcher’s note:

Discovery log:

I'd suggest capitalizing 'note' and 'log' here.

I want to critique the whole species and common name, but I don't know enough to say what's correct for that part I think.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
First SCP!

This is a rework of an article I wrote in December that was greenlit by Popsioak on wikidot and SanttilaThePun on IRC.

Huge shout out to Phiiota, Placeholder McD, Ralliston, Farcical, Roget, Grigori Karpin, caspian2, ThisMightBeAuto, EstrellaYoshte, Riemann, Elunerazim, and Bufoh for being this draft's critters*! Also thanks to JakdragonX and cybersqyd for crittering the draft back in December!

  • Critters in no particular order.
